query: Tell me about Greta Gerwig's filmmaking universe

songs.title: Barbie World
songs.artist: Nicki Minaj & Ice Spice with Aqua

videos.title: Greta Gerwig and Saoirse Ronan on the Importance of Female Voices in Hollywood
videos.evidence: Gerwig and Ronan discuss how Lady Bird captures the specific emotional landscape of adolescence — the urgency of wanting to leave home and the painful beauty of realizing what you're leaving behind.

videos.title: Saoirse Ronan and Greta Gerwig talk Lady Bird
videos.evidence: Ronan describes how Lady Bird's coming-of-age story resonated with her own experience of growing up between countries and identities — the universal feeling of wanting to be anywhere other than where you are.

videos.title: Saoirse Ronan Rewatches Little Women, Lady Bird, Atonement & More
videos.evidence: Ronan revisits her performances in Gerwig's films, reflecting on how each role captured a different stage of growing up — from Lady Bird's high school rebellion to Jo March's artistic coming of age.

videos.title: Greta Gerwig on 'Little Women' | Amanpour and Company
videos.evidence: Gerwig discusses her approach to adapting Little Women through an explicitly female perspective — reframing Alcott's novel not as a quaint period piece but as a radical statement about women's artistic and economic independence.

videos.title: How I Wrote Little Women — Greta Gerwig's Writing Advice
videos.evidence: Gerwig reveals her screenwriting process for Little Women, explaining how she restructured Alcott's chronological narrative into a nonlinear dialogue between youth and adulthood — a structural innovation driven by her perspective as a woman filmmaker.

videos.title: Greta Gerwig and Margot Robbie Discuss Barbie's Surprising Feminism
videos.evidence: Gerwig and Robbie discuss how Barbie uses the world's most famous doll to explore what it means to be a woman — turning a corporate IP into a vehicle for genuine feminist inquiry about identity, autonomy, and the impossible standards of femininity.

videos.title: How I Wrote Little Women — Greta Gerwig's Writing Advice
videos.evidence: Gerwig discusses her innovative approach to adapting Alcott: interweaving past and present timelines, using the novel's own publication history as a framing device, and giving Jo's ambitions the same structural weight as her romantic life.

videos.title: Greta Gerwig Interview — Little Women (2019)
videos.evidence: Gerwig explains why she felt compelled to adapt Little Women despite seven previous film versions — the novel's themes of female ambition and artistic compromise felt urgently contemporary to her.

videos.title: Greta Gerwig and Noah Baumbach Talk 'Frances Ha' and Falling In Love
videos.evidence: Gerwig and Baumbach discuss how Frances Ha balances genuine emotional vulnerability with comedy — Frances's failures are funny precisely because they matter to her so deeply.

videos.title: 73 Questions With Barbie's Greta Gerwig | Vogue
videos.evidence: Gerwig demonstrates her natural ability to toggle between levity and depth — her rapid-fire answers reveal both her comedic instincts and the emotional intelligence that makes her films resonate beyond their humor.

videos.title: LADY BIRD Director and Cast Q&A | TIFF 2017
videos.evidence: Gerwig discusses why she set Lady Bird in Sacramento — a city rarely depicted in cinema — and how the specificity of place (particular streets, particular light, particular trees) gives the film its emotional authenticity.

videos.title: Greta Gerwig Writes What She Knows (and She Knows a Lot)
videos.evidence: An analysis of how Gerwig uses autobiography and specific geographical detail — Sacramento in Lady Bird, New York in Frances Ha — to ground her stories in the lived texture of particular places.

videos.title: Greta Gerwig and Noah Baumbach Talk 'Frances Ha' and Falling In Love
videos.evidence: Gerwig and Baumbach describe their co-writing process for Frances Ha — how they developed the screenplay together while falling in love, blurring the line between creative and romantic partnership.

videos.title: Timothée Chalamet & Saoirse Ronan Reunite After 'Lady Bird' and 'Little Women'
videos.evidence: Chalamet and Ronan reflect on working with Gerwig across two films, describing how she builds a creative environment where actors feel safe enough to be vulnerable — the foundation of the performances that earned both Oscar nominations.

videos.title: Greta Gerwig & James Cameron | Directors on Directors
videos.evidence: Gerwig discusses her approach to collaboration with James Cameron — two directors with vastly different scales but a shared commitment to building creative teams that elevate the work beyond any single vision.

discoveryGroups.title: Her Films
discoveryGroups.description: The five films Greta Gerwig has written and directed.

cards.title: Frances Ha
cards.context: Gerwig and Baumbach's black-and-white love letter to New York, following a dancer navigating the gap between ambition and reality.

cards.title: Lady Bird
cards.context: Gerwig's directorial debut — a coming-of-age masterpiece set in Sacramento that earned five Academy Award nominations.

cards.title: Little Women
cards.context: Gerwig's nonlinear reimagining of Louisa May Alcott's novel — six Oscar nominations including Best Picture and Best Adapted Screenplay.

cards.title: Barbie
cards.context: The cultural phenomenon that made Gerwig the highest-grossing female director in history — a subversive feminist comedy inside a candy-colored blockbuster.

cards.title: Louisa May Alcott
cards.context: The novelist whose Little Women provided Gerwig with the source material for her most structurally ambitious film.

cards.title: French New Wave
cards.context: The movement that inspired Frances Ha's black-and-white cinematography, restless energy, and literary sensibility.

cards.title: Mumblecore
cards.context: The independent film movement where Gerwig cut her teeth as an actress before transitioning to directing.

discoveryGroups.title: The Cast
discoveryGroups.description: The actors who bring Gerwig's characters to life across her films.

cards.title: Saoirse Ronan
cards.context: Gerwig's closest collaborator and muse — Lady Bird McPherson and Jo March across two Oscar-nominated performances.

cards.title: Timothée Chalamet
cards.context: Kyle Scheible and Laurie Laurence — two very different young men, both capturing Gerwig's gift for writing male vulnerability.

cards.title: Margot Robbie
cards.context: Both star and producer of Barbie — Robbie championed Gerwig as director and delivered a performance that balanced plastic perfection with genuine existential crisis.

cards.title: Florence Pugh
cards.context: Pugh's Amy March was a revelation — transforming the novel's least sympathetic sister into its most complex, earning a Best Supporting Actress nomination.

cards.title: Laura Dern
cards.context: Marmee March — the moral center of Alcott's novel, played by Dern with warmth, intelligence, and quiet feminist resolve.

cards.title: Laurie Metcalf
cards.context: Marion McPherson — the mother-daughter dynamic at Lady Bird's heart earned Metcalf an Oscar nomination for Best Supporting Actress.

cards.title: Ryan Gosling
cards.context: Ken — Gosling's committed, hilarious performance and Oscar-nominated 'I'm Just Ken' became Barbie's breakout element.

cards.title: Emma Watson
cards.context: Meg March — Watson brought quiet dignity to the eldest sister, whose choice of domesticity over ambition is treated as no less valid than Jo's literary career.

cards.title: Beanie Feldstein
cards.context: Julie Steffans — Lady Bird's best friend, whose quiet loyalty and heartbreak provide the film's most tender subplot.

cards.title: Lucas Hedges
cards.context: Danny O'Neill — Lady Bird's first boyfriend, whose secret drives one of the film's most compassionately handled revelations.

cards.title: Tracy Letts
cards.context: Larry McPherson — the quiet, depressed father whose gentle support contrasts with Marion's tough love.

discoveryGroups.title: Behind the Scenes
discoveryGroups.description: The writers, composers, and crew who built Gerwig's visual and sonic worlds.

cards.title: Noah Baumbach
cards.meta: Co-Writer · Frances Ha, Mistress America, Barbie
cards.context: Gerwig's creative and life partner — their co-writing produced Frances Ha, Mistress America, and Barbie's screenplay.

cards.title: Jon Brion
cards.context: The composer whose delicate, emotionally precise score gave Lady Bird its musical soul.

cards.title: Alexandre Desplat
cards.context: The Oscar-winning composer whose score for Little Women weaves between the warmth of childhood and the melancholy of its passing.

cards.title: Mark Ronson
cards.context: The Grammy-winning producer who curated and produced Barbie's blockbuster soundtrack, featuring Dua Lipa, Billie Eilish, and Ryan Gosling.

cards.title: Sam Levy
cards.context: Gerwig's DP on Frances Ha and Lady Bird — his naturalistic, luminous cinematography defined her visual style.

cards.title: Rodrigo Prieto
cards.context: The Oscar-nominated cinematographer who gave Barbie its hyper-saturated, candy-colored visual identity.

cards.title: Jacqueline Durran
cards.context: Won the Academy Award for Best Costume Design for Little Women — her period costumes captured character through textile and silhouette.

cards.title: Nick Houy
cards.context: Gerwig's editor on Barbie, whose pacing balanced the film's frenetic comedy with its surprisingly emotional climax.

cards.title: Sarah Flack
cards.meta: Editor · Frances Ha, Lady Bird, Little Women
cards.context: Gerwig's primary editor across three films — Flack's rhythmic cuts give Gerwig's dialogue its distinctive musicality.

discoveryGroups.title: Soundtracks & Scores
discoveryGroups.description: The music that defines Gerwig's films — from Jon Brion's delicate Lady Bird score to Barbie's chart-topping soundtrack.

cards.title: Jon Brion
cards.context: Scored Lady Bird with a delicate, emotionally precise approach that mirrors the film's balance of comedy and heartbreak.

cards.title: Alexandre Desplat
cards.context: The two-time Oscar-winning composer (The Grand Budapest Hotel, The Shape of Water) who brought classical elegance to Little Women's period setting.

rawResponse.label: Raw Claude 3.5 Sonnet
rawResponse.text: A basic response with no verified entity data, no cross-media connections, and no actionable discovery links.

enhancedResponse.label: UnitedTribes Enhanced
enhancedResponse.text: Rich response with 28 verified entities, cross-media connections spanning film, literature, and music, plus actionable discovery links from authorized partnerships.
